title: Heaps
description: BinaryHeap is Rust's priority queue — a max-heap by default. Wrap values in Reverse for a min-heap. Master push, pop, peek, and the top-K pattern to handle priority-based interview problems efficiently.

sections.title: BinaryHeap (max-heap by default)
sections.explanation: **BinaryHeap<T>** is a max-heap: `.push()` inserts, `.pop()` removes and returns the largest element (as Option<T>), and `.peek()` views the max without removing. All operations are O(log n). Create from a Vec with `BinaryHeap::from(vec)` or push one at a time.

exercise.prompt: Create a BinaryHeap and push values 10, 30, 20, 50, 40. Peek at the max, then pop elements one at a time and print them (should come out in descending order).

validation.message: Use BinaryHeap::new(), push, peek, and pop for heap operations

exercise.hint: BinaryHeap is a max-heap, so pop always returns the largest remaining element.

sections.title: Min-heap with Reverse
sections.explanation: Rust's BinaryHeap is max-only, but wrapping elements in `std::cmp::Reverse(val)` inverts the ordering to create a **min-heap**. Push `Reverse(val)`, pop returns `Reverse(val)` — extract with `.0`. This pattern appears in Dijkstra's algorithm, merge-K-lists, and median finding.

exercise.prompt: Create a min-heap using Reverse. Push values 50, 10, 30, 20, 40. Pop all elements and print them (should come out in ascending order).

validation.message: Wrap values in Reverse() and destructure with Reverse(val) when popping

exercise.hint: Reverse inverts the ordering so the smallest value has the highest priority.

sections.title: Top-K elements pattern
sections.explanation: To find the **K largest** elements: use a min-heap of size K — push each element, and if the heap exceeds K, pop the smallest. After processing all elements, the heap contains the K largest. For **K smallest**, use a max-heap with the same logic. This runs in O(n log K).

exercise.prompt: Complete the top_k_largest function that finds the K largest elements. Test with nums = [10, 4, 3, 8, 7, 1, 9] and k = 3. Print the sorted result.

validation.message: Push Reverse(num), evict when size exceeds k with pop()

exercise.hint: Call .pop() to evict the smallest when heap grows beyond k.

sections.title: Building a heap from a Vec
sections.explanation: Use `BinaryHeap::from(vec)` to convert a Vec into a heap in O(n) time — faster than pushing one by one (O(n log n)). The resulting heap can be drained in sorted order with repeated `.pop()` calls, which is essentially heapsort.

exercise.prompt: Create a BinaryHeap from vec![7, 2, 5, 1, 8, 3]. Peek at the max. Drain the heap into a Vec and print it (descending order).

validation.message: Max is 8, draining gives descending order: [8,7,5,3,2,1]
Use BinaryHeap::from() to build, pop() to drain in order

exercise.hint: Call .pop() in the while let loop to drain elements in descending order.
